title: Retail store design
paperName: Paper 3 - Specialist Options: Approaches, Issues and Debates
summary: 9990 Consumer — layout, flow, signage, and environmental psychology in retail spaces.
learningObjectives: Explain how store layout influences customer movement.
Analyse grid, free-flow, and loop designs.
Evaluate signage, lighting, and product placement.
Apply studies on wayfinding and dwell time.

simpleExplanation.title: Design that sells
simpleExplanation.summary: Store layout guides where customers walk, what they see, and how long they stay — shaping purchase behaviour without a word spoken.

flashcards.back: Parallel aisles in a predictable pattern — efficient for known-item shopping (supermarkets).
Asymmetric paths encouraging exploration — increases dwell time and impulse browsing (boutiques).
Single guided path past all departments — maximises product exposure (IKEA-style).
First 5–15 feet inside entrance — customers slow down and adjust; prime display space.
High-impact wall display to the right of entrance — captures first visual attention.
Layout combines with lighting, scent, and colour (atmospherics) to shape approach behaviour.

sections.content: **Retail store design** (2.1.1) opens Consumer Psychology. Link **environment** to **behaviour** with named studies.

sections.question: A fashion retailer replaces its grid layout with a free-flow design, widens aisles, and places bestsellers on a power wall to the right of the entrance. Sales rise 12% in three months. Using retail design psychology, explain these changes and evaluate their effectiveness.
sections.solution:
**Apply — layout types:** **Free-flow** removes rigid aisles, encouraging **exploration** and longer **dwell time** — shoppers encounter more products incidentally. The **power wall** (right of entrance) exploits the tendency to look right on entry, placing **high-margin bestsellers** in the first visual field. Wider aisles reduce **crowding stress** (link 2.2.1), supporting **approach behaviour**.

**Apply — wayfinding:** Clear **signage** and sight lines reduce frustration; customers who navigate easily stay longer (Underhill's *Why We Buy* observations on path tracking).

**Evaluate — strengths:** Field evidence (12% sales rise) supports **ecological validity** — real shoppers, real purchases. Free-flow suits **high-involvement browsing** (2.3.1) where discovery matters.

**Evaluate — limitations:** **Individual differences** — some shoppers prefer efficient grid layouts for goal-directed trips. **Confounding variables** — simultaneous marketing or seasonal stock may explain sales rise. **Ethical issue** — deliberate path manipulation may exploit **low-involvement** shoppers without informed consent. **Cross-cultural** validity uncertain — layout norms vary internationally.
